title: SEGMENT 12 — Quality scores, Phred scaling, and what Q30 really means

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Quality scores, Phred scaling, and what Q30 really means
When you get your data back from an Illumina run, every single base call comes with a number attached to it — a quality score — telling you how confident the base caller is that the call was correct.
This score is one of the most important pieces of information in the entire data file, and almost every downstream analysis tool uses it heavily.
We need to spend a segment on what these scores mean and how to read them, because they show up everywhere.
The quality score Illumina uses is called the Phred score, named after a much older base-calling program called Phred that was originally developed in the late 1990s for Sanger sequencing data.
The Phred score, abbreviated Q, is logarithmically related to the probability of error in the base call.
The formula is:
Q equals negative ten times the base-ten logarithm of P, where P is the probability that the base call is wrong.
Let me unpack that with concrete numbers, because the log scaling is where most people lose intuition.
A Phred score of 10 means a one-in-ten chance of error, or 10 percent error rate.
A Phred score of 20 means a one-in-a-hundred chance, or 1 percent error.
A Phred score of 30 means one-in-a-thousand, 0.1 percent error.
A Phred score of 40 means one-in-ten-thousand.
Each ten units of Phred is one decimal order of magnitude in error probability.
The higher the Q, the more confident the call.

Q30 — one error in a thousand — is the standard threshold for "high-quality" base calls in most pipelines, which is why you'll hear people talking about the percentage of bases above Q30 as a quality metric for a sequencing run.
A good Illumina run typically has more than 80 percent of bases above Q30, often 90 percent or more.
Here's the analogy.
A Phred quality score is like a confidence rating on a weather forecast.
Q10 means "I'm 90 percent sure it'll be sunny" — there's a noticeable chance you'll get rained on.
Q20 means "99 percent sure" — basically reliable but not guaranteed.
Q30 means "99.9 percent sure" — you'd plan a wedding around it.
Q40 means "99.99 percent sure" — you can essentially treat it as certain for any practical purpose.
The log scaling matches how we intuitively reason about probabilities of rare events: each extra decimal of confidence costs about the same amount of effort to achieve.
How does the instrument actually assign quality scores?
In the early days, Illumina used hand-tuned base-calling algorithms that estimated quality from the intensity, sharpness, and color purity of each cluster signal.

The current generation uses machine-learning models — neural networks trained on huge datasets of real reads with known correct sequences — that look at the raw signals in the context of surrounding cycles and predict the probability of error for each base.
These ML-based base callers have substantially improved both accuracy and the calibration of quality scores over the last several years.
A modern Illumina Q30 call really is correct about 999 times out of 1000, as a well-calibrated quality score should be.
Quality scores are stored in the data files using a notation called ASCII offset encoding.
Each quality score is converted to a printable character — Q30 becomes the character ?, Q40 becomes I, and so on — by adding 33 to the score and looking up the corresponding ASCII character.
This is what produces those weird-looking strings of seemingly random punctuation in FASTQ files, the standard Illumina output format.
Every base in the FASTQ has its corresponding quality character right below it.
If you're a bioinformatician you stop noticing the encoding after a while; if you're not, those FASTQ files look like alphabet soup, but every character is a calibrated probability.
What do you do with quality scores?
Several things.
Filtering: variant callers and aligners typically apply a quality cutoff, ignoring bases below some threshold like Q20.

Recalibration: for clinical variant calling, the GATK pipeline includes a step called base quality score recalibration, which adjusts the instrument's reported quality scores against known true-call sets to correct for systematic biases.

Variant call confidence: when calling a variant, the underlying base qualities contribute to the variant's confidence score, so a variant supported by many high-quality reads scores higher than one supported by a few low-quality reads.

Trimming: most analyses include a step that trims the low-quality tails off the ends of reads, because the last few bases of an Illumina read are often noticeably worse than the middle — that dephasing problem again.
The standard tool, called Trimmomatic or fastp, uses a sliding-window quality calculation to clip off the bad ends while keeping the good middle.
The point of quality scores is that they let you reason quantitatively about confidence.
Without them, a base call is just a letter; with them, a base call is a calibrated probability distribution, and downstream tools can propagate that uncertainty through the entire analysis.

The single most useful piece of metadata in sequencing data is the quality score, and getting it right is one of the reasons Illumina has been so dominant: their quality scores are reliable, well-calibrated, and consistent across instruments and runs.
Other platforms have struggled with quality calibration; Illumina, in the modern era, has it nailed.
